scoreBands.label: Compliant
scoreBands.headline: Your organisation demonstrates strong AI governance maturity

scoreBands.label: Partially Compliant
scoreBands.headline: Governance foundations exist — critical gaps need remediation

scoreBands.label: Emerging
scoreBands.headline: Significant AI governance gaps identified — immediate action needed

scoreBands.label: At Risk
scoreBands.headline: Organisation is exposed to material regulatory and operational AI risk

sections.title: AI Inventory & Discovery
sections.description: Tracks whether the company knows which AI tools are in use, including shadow AI

questions.text: Does your company maintain a documented register of all AI tools in use — including free, trial, and employee-initiated tools (e.g. ChatGPT, Copilot, Gemini)?
questions.guidance: A complete AI tool inventory is the foundation of all governance. Without it, compliance is impossible to demonstrate.

questions.text: Are there AI tools being used by employees that IT or compliance is NOT aware of (shadow AI)?

options.label: Yes — we know shadow AI exists

questions.guidance: Shadow AI is the #1 risk flag in regulatory inspections. This question is reverse-scored — the 'safer' answer is No.

questions.text: Which departments use AI tools most heavily? (Select all that apply)
questions.scoring: informational — flags high-risk departments for section breakdown
questions.guidance: Finance, HR, and Legal AI use triggers high-risk classification under EU AI Act Art. 6.

questions.text: Are any AI tools used in credit decisioning, fraud detection, or customer risk scoring?

options.label: Yes, with full documentation and controls

questions.guidance: Credit and fraud AI is explicitly classified as high-risk under EU AI Act Art. 6 and requires extensive documentation under RBI's ML model risk circular.

questions.text: Does your company have a formal policy defining which AI tools are approved for use?

options.label: Yes, written and communicated to all staff

questions.guidance: A written AI acceptable use policy is a baseline requirement for both RBI IT governance audits and EU AI Act compliance.

questions.text: How frequently is the AI tool inventory reviewed or updated?
questions.guidance: AI tool landscapes change fast. Quarterly minimum review is recommended. Never reviewing = automatic audit finding.

sections.title: Data Governance & Privacy
sections.description: Assesses how customer and company data is handled within AI tools

questions.text: Do employees paste or upload customer PII (names, phone numbers, Aadhaar, PAN, financial data) into AI tools?
questions.guidance: Pasting PII into external AI tools (ChatGPT, Gemini etc.) likely violates DPDP Act S.4 and RBI data localisation requirements. This is the most common compliance breach in Indian fintechs today.

questions.text: Do your AI vendor agreements include data processing clauses specifying the vendor will NOT train on your company's data?

options.label: Yes, explicit clause in all AI vendor contracts

questions.guidance: Without an explicit no-training clause, your customer data may be used to improve the vendor's models — a serious breach of DPDP consent requirements.

questions.text: Is customer data processed by AI tools stored on servers outside India?

options.label: No — all processing stays in India
Yes — data goes to overseas servers

questions.guidance: RBI's data localisation circular requires payment and customer financial data to be stored in India. Cross-border AI processing without safeguards violates this.

questions.text: Has your company conducted a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) for any AI system that processes personal data?

options.label: Yes, for all relevant AI systems

questions.guidance: DPIAs are expected under DPDP Act for significant data processing and mandatory under EU AI Act for high-risk systems.

questions.text: Are there technical controls (DLP, endpoint policies) that prevent employees from pasting sensitive data into external AI tools?
questions.guidance: Policy without enforcement is a finding waiting to happen. DLP rules blocking AI site uploads are the gold standard.

sections.title: Human Oversight & Accountability
sections.description: Evaluates whether humans are meaningfully in control of AI decisions

questions.text: Is there a named individual or team responsible for AI governance in your company?

options.label: Yes, named role with clear mandate

questions.guidance: EU AI Act requires designated human oversight. An unnamed responsibility is no responsibility at all in an audit.

questions.text: For AI systems that make or influence decisions (loan approvals, hiring, fraud flags), is there a mandatory human review step before action is taken?

options.label: Yes, always — no AI decision is fully automated
Depends on the system / case by case
No — AI decisions are acted on directly

questions.guidance: Fully automated consequential decisions are prohibited under EU AI Act Art. 14 and against RBI guidance on model risk management.

questions.text: Can customers or employees request an explanation for decisions made by AI systems?
questions.guidance: DPDP Act S.12 gives data principals the right to information about automated decisions. EU AI Act Art. 13 mandates transparency for high-risk systems.

questions.text: Are AI model outputs reviewed periodically for accuracy, bias, or performance drift?

options.label: Yes, formal monitoring with documented results

questions.guidance: Model drift is a top cause of AI-related financial harm. RBI expects documented model validation cycles. No monitoring = unacceptable risk.

questions.text: Does your board or senior leadership receive any reporting on AI risk or AI tool usage?

options.label: Yes, regular board-level AI risk reporting

questions.guidance: Board awareness of AI risk is increasingly expected in governance frameworks. This is a maturity indicator.

sections.title: Employee Awareness & Training
sections.description: Checks whether staff are equipped to use AI safely and responsibly

questions.text: Have employees received any formal training on responsible AI use (data handling, bias awareness, prompt safety)?
questions.guidance: EU AI Act Art. 4 explicitly requires AI literacy for staff working with AI systems. Untrained employees are your biggest prompt-risk surface.

questions.text: Are employees required to acknowledge an AI usage policy before using company-approved AI tools?

options.label: Policy exists but no formal sign-off

questions.guidance: Unacknowledged policies don't hold up in audits. A digital sign-off trail is a minimal requirement.

questions.text: Do employees know which AI tools are approved vs prohibited for work use?

options.label: Yes, clearly communicated with a published list

questions.guidance: If employees don't know what's allowed, they'll use whatever works. Approved tool lists must be published and accessible.

questions.text: Is AI literacy training part of new employee onboarding?
questions.guidance: Embedding AI governance in onboarding is a sign of a mature programme. New hires are the highest prompt-risk group.

sections.title: Vendor & Third-party AI Risk
sections.description: Examines how third-party AI embedded in SaaS tools is being governed

questions.text: Has your company reviewed the AI sub-processors used by your SaaS vendors (e.g. does your CRM use OpenAI under the hood)?
questions.guidance: Your Salesforce or Zendesk may be sending customer data to OpenAI. Most companies don't know. DPDP Act makes the data controller liable regardless.

questions.text: Do vendor contracts specify what happens to your data if an AI vendor is acquired, shuts down, or changes their data policy?

options.label: Yes, exit/change clauses in all contracts

questions.guidance: AI vendors are acquired and pivoted constantly. Without exit clauses, you have no data rights if the vendor changes hands.

questions.text: Are AI vendors used by your company SOC 2 Type II certified or equivalent?
questions.guidance: SOC 2 Type II is the baseline security certification for SaaS vendors. Using uncertified AI vendors is an audit finding.

questions.text: Is there a process to evaluate new AI tools before employees are permitted to use them?

options.label: Yes, formal approval process with documented criteria
No process — employees can use any tool

questions.guidance: Without a tool approval process, shadow AI is inevitable. This is the preventative control that stops Q2 from being a problem.

sections.title: Incident & Risk Management
sections.description: Assesses readiness to detect, respond to, and report AI-related incidents

questions.text: Has your company experienced any AI-related incident in the last 12 months (data leak via prompt, biased output, model failure, vendor breach)?
questions.guidance: Prior incidents must be documented and reported. Undisclosed significant incidents are a regulatory liability.

questions.text: Is there a defined process for reporting and escalating AI-related incidents?

options.label: Yes, documented with escalation paths and timelines

questions.guidance: EU AI Act requires serious incident reporting within 15 days. Without a process, you'll miss this deadline and compound the problem.

questions.text: Does your company have a documented AI risk register?

options.label: AI risks captured in general IT risk register

questions.guidance: AI risks have characteristics distinct from general IT risks (bias, opacity, emergent behaviour). A standalone register demonstrates maturity.

questions.text: Is AI risk assessed as part of your annual internal audit or external audit cycle?

options.label: Yes, AI explicitly scoped into audits
Informally discussed but not formally scoped
AI not on the audit agenda

questions.guidance: Regulators are beginning to expect AI to appear in audit scopes. Being ahead of this is a credibility signal.

sections.title: Transparency & Explainability
sections.description: Tests whether the company can demonstrate AI governance to regulators, auditors, and customers

questions.text: If asked by a regulator today to produce an AI usage report — which tools, which data, which decisions — how long would it take your team?
questions.guidance: This is the single most important question. Everything else in this scorecard is infrastructure for answering 'yes, less than a day.'

questions.text: Does your company disclose to customers when AI is used to make decisions that affect them?

options.label: Yes, clear disclosure in all relevant customer journeys
We do not use AI in customer-facing decisions

questions.guidance: Non-disclosure of AI use in financial decisions violates RBI's Fair Practice Code and EU AI Act Art. 50. Customer trust and regulatory standing both depend on this.

questions.text: Are AI model cards or system documentation maintained for internally built or fine-tuned AI models?

options.label: We do not build or fine-tune models

questions.guidance: Model cards capture purpose, training data, performance metrics, and known limitations. Required for EU AI Act high-risk systems.

questions.text: Can your company demonstrate to an auditor that AI outputs are logged and traceable?

options.label: Yes, logs maintained with timestamps and decision context

questions.guidance: Audit trail for AI decisions is mandatory under EU AI Act Art. 12 and expected under RBI's IT audit guidelines. No logs = no defence.
